title: Optimal Quantum Illumination with Nonlocal Non-Gaussian Operations
authors: Luis D. Zambrano Palma, Yusef Maleki, M. Suhail Zubairy
abstract: Enhancing quantum illumination with highly entangled probes remains an active area of research. In this context, non-Gaussian operations provide an effective route for engineering probe states that can surpass the standard two-mode squeezed state (TMSS). In this work, we investigate a specific nonlocal non-Gaussian operation protocol and show that the engineered state using this protocol outperforms previously considered local non-Gaussian scenarios, engineered based on photon catalysis, addition, and subtraction under realistic conditions, including photon loss. Furthermore, by employing a $50{:}50$ beam splitter with photon-number difference detection, we demonstrate a significant enhancement in the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for target detection relative to the TMSS. Thus, our protocol exhibits improved performance, highlighting a resource-efficient and experimentally feasible probe for enhanced quantum illumination.
summary: The paper proposes a nonlocal non-Gaussian photon addition protocol to enhance quantum illumination. It demonstrates that this method provides a significant advantage in target detection sensitivity and is more robust to photon loss and more resource-efficient than existing local non-Gaussian methods.
main_problem: Enhancing the performance of Quantum Illumination (QI) for detecting weakly reflecting targets in noisy environments by improving upon standard Two-Mode Squeezed State (TMSS) and existing local non-Gaussian protocols.
main_result: The proposed Nonlocal Non-Gaussian Photon Addition (NLPA) protocol outperforms local non-Gaussian operations (PS, PA, PC) and the TMSS baseline, offering a significant SNR enhancement and superior robustness to photon loss while maintaining high success probabilities.
method: The authors use continuous-variable quantum information theory, evaluating error probabilities via the Helstrom limit and Quantum Chernoff Bound, and benchmarking protocols using the error exponent ratio (gain parameter) and von Neumann entropy.
model_or_system: A bipartite quantum illumination system consisting of a signal mode sent to a weakly reflecting target (low transmissivity kappa) and a retained idler mode, interacting with a thermal background environment.
key_observables: Error probability (Pe), Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), von Neumann entropy (Ev), success probability (Psucc), and the gain parameter (G).
important_parameters: Target reflectivity (kappa), thermal photon number (n_th), squeezing parameter (r), beam splitter transmissivity (T), and photon loss (eta).
main_assumptions: The target reflectivity is assumed to be very low (kappa << 1), the environment is modeled as a thermal state, and the large-K limit is approximated using the Quantum Chernoff Bound.
figures_summary: Fig 1 & 2 show protocol schematics; Fig 3 shows error probability vs. number of copies; Fig 4 shows gain ratio vs. transmissivity; Fig 6 shows error probability under different regimes; Fig 7 shows gain vs. transmissivity in lossless cases; Fig 8 & 9 show performance and gain under photon loss.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QI problem, describes the NLPA and local non-Gaussian protocols, quantifies entanglement and success probability, compares detection performance (error probability and gain) in lossless and lossy regimes, and evaluates different receiver strategies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how nonlocal non-Gaussian operations can engineer robust entanglement for sensing tasks in the presence of decoherence and loss.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Conditional state preparation via beam-splitter interac- tion. (b) State preparation using local non-Gaussian operations with TMSS input.

FIG. 2. Schematic representation of the QI protocol using NLPA. (a) State preparation: a TMSS source generates signal (B) and idler (A) modes that interact with auxiliary Fock states |m⟩and |n⟩through beam splitters BSA and BS(θ), followed by conditional detection of |m′⟩and |n′⟩, producing the non-Gaussian transmitter. (b) Target sensing: the signal mode propagates toward a weakly reflecting target characterized by transmissivity κ ≪1 in a noisy environment, while the idler mode is retained locally. (c) Measurement stage: The returned mode and the stored idler are interfered on a 50:50 beam splitter BS1. Photon-number measurements are performed at both outputs, and their difference ˆMBS =...

FIG. 3. (a) Von Neumann entropy EV of the reduced state ρB and (b) Success probability Psucc as a function of the beam splitter trans- missivity T. Error probability as fuction of number of copies K (c) evaluated at the transmissivity T that maximizes the von Neumann entropy EV and (d) transmissivity T that maximizes the success prob- ability for each protocol using one auxiliary photon. The TMSS case is shown as a reference.

FIG. 4. Error exponent ratio G as a function of transmissivity T using one auxiliary photon. Panels correspond to the ratio of (a) nonlocal photon addition (NLPA), (b) local photon addition (PA), (c) local photon catalysis (PC), and (d) local photon subtraction (PS).

FIG. 5. (a) Von Neumann entropy EV of the reduced state ρB and (b) Success probability Psucc as a function of the beam splitter trans- missivity T. Error probability as fuction of number of copies K (c) evaluated at the transmissivity T that maximizes the von Neumann entropy EV and (d) transmissivity T that maximizes the success prob- ability for each protocol using two auxiliary photons. The TMSS case is shown as a reference.
